section.p: Calculate the amount for any adjustments youyou and your spouse took in .

If you were legally responsible for paying the interest on a qualified student loan, you could have deducted up to in interest payments. The loan must have been for you, your spouse, or your dependent.
This includes both required and voluntarily pre-paid interest payments.

p.a: Learn more about student loan interest. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: Student loan interest you you and your spouse paid in

section.p: If you were a K-12 educator, you could have deducted up to of unreimbursed expenses in . If you filed a joint tax return and both of you were educators, you could have deducted up to .
Qualified expenses includes:

ul.li: Books, supplies, computer equipment (including related software and services)
Other equipment and supplementary materials that you used in the classroom

p.a: Learn more about educator expenses. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: Educator expenses youyou and your spouse paid in

section.p: If youyou or your spouse contributed to a traditional (pre-tax) IRA outside of your payroll contributions in , you may have been able to claim a deduction.
Don't include amounts that were already included on one of your jobs, contributions to Roth IRAs, or contributions to other retirement plans.

p.a: Learn more about IRA contributions. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: Traditional (pre-tax) IRA contributions youyou and your spouse made in

section.p: If youyou or your spouse were self employed and made contributions to qualified retirement plans in , you may have been eligible for the self-employed retirement plan deduction.
To qualify, youyou or your spouse must have:

ul.li: Been a working partner in a partnership, or

section.p: Eligible plans may have included:

ul.li: Savings Incentive Match Plan for Employees

section.p: Don't include Individual Retirement Arrangement (IRA) contributions.
You must have used a special rule to calculate retirement plan contributions for yourself.

p.a: Learn more about self-employed retirement contributions. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: Self-employed retirement contributions youyou and your spouse made in

section.p: If youyou or your spouse contributed to an HSA outside of your payroll contributions in , you could have claimed a deduction.
Don't include amounts that were already included on one of your jobs.

p.a: Learn more about HSA contributions. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: HSA contributions youyou or your spouse made in

section.p: If you were divorced or separated, you could have deducted alimony paid in to a spouse or a former spouse under a divorce or separation instrument made before 2019.

p.a: Learn more about alimony. (opens in a new tab)

section.h3: Penalty for early withdrawal of savings
section.p: If youyou or your spouse withdrew funds from a certificate of deposit or other deferred interest account before maturity, you may have been charged a penalty by your financial institution in .

p.a: Learn more about penalties for early withdrawals. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: Penalty amount youyou and your spouse paid in

section.h3: Military and intelligence community moving expenses
section.p: You could have deducted unreimbursed moving expenses paid in if:

ul.li: You wereYou or your spouse were a member of the Armed Forces on active duty and your move was due to a military order and a permanent change of station, or
You wereYou or your spouse were an employee or new appointee of the intelligence community and your move was due to a change in assignment which required relocation

p.a: Learn more about military and intelligence community moving expenses. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: Moving expenses youyou and your spouse paid in

section.p: If you wereyou or your spouse were an Armed Forces reservist, qualified performing artist, fee-basis state or local government official, or employee with a disability and impairment-related work expenses, you could have deducted expenses in that were:

ul.li: Common and accepted in your field of trade, business, or profession
Helpful and appropriate for your business

p.a: Learn more about certain business expenses. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: Eligible business expenses youyou and your spouse paid in

section.p: If you wereyou or your spouse were self employed and had a net profit for the year, you could have deducted your health insurance premiums.

Eligible premiums were medical, dental, vision, and long-term care insurance. The policy could be for yourself, your spouse, and your dependents. The policy could also cover your child who was under the age of 27 at the end of the year, even if the child wasn't your dependent.

p.a: Learn more about self-employed health insurance. (opens in a new tab)

fg-set.question: Health insurance premiums youyou and your spouse paid in

fg-alert.heading: You don't qualify for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC)
fg-alert.p: Based on your adjusted gross income (including the adjustments you entered), you don’t qualify for the EITC for .
For more information, see Publication 596, Earned Income Credit.

modal-content.p: Adjustments are expenses, payments, contributions, and fees that you may have been able to subtract from your total income to reduce how much of your income got taxed. They are a type of deduction.
